title: Other History
intro: "Other History" gathers quizbowl questions on history outside the standalone United States, Europe, and world-history-survey buckets, and its distribution skews heavily toward the ancient world. Mesopotamia, Egypt, Greece, and Rome together account for the largest share of answerlines, with recurring names such as Carthage, Sparta, Alexander the Great, Julius Caesar, and Hadrian appearing alongside cities like Babylon, Nineveh, and Troy and material-culture staples like cuneiform, bronze, and terracotta. Persian and Near Eastern material spans the Achaemenid kings Cyrus the Great and Darius I through the Sassanid Empire and the rise of Islam under Muhammad.

Beyond the Mediterranean world, East Asian history concentrates on the sequence of Chinese dynasties, from Shang oracle bones through the Qin, Han, and Ming, alongside Silk Road trade and twentieth-century figures like Deng Xiaoping. South Asian questions cluster around Ashoka, the Indus Valley Civilization, and Sri Lanka, while African answerlines range from ancient Nubia and the Garamantes to modern Nigeria and South Africa. Pre-Columbian and Latin American history is anchored by Mesoamerican and Andean civilizations -- the Olmecs, Aztec Empire, Teotihuacan, and Nazca -- alongside colonial and modern topics such as the Falklands War and Cuba.

A substantial cluster of questions concerns Australia, New Zealand, and the wider Pacific: Maori history and the Treaty of Waitangi, Aboriginal Australians, and the politics of modern Australia and New Zealand recur often, as do Canadian topics such as confederation-era figures and the peoples of the Arctic. Twentieth-century Europe is represented mainly through the World Wars, the Soviet Union, and Vladimir Lenin.

Finally, a distinct thematic cluster consists of common-link commodity and material-culture answerlines -- metals like tin, iron, and gold; crops like tea, rice, and coffee; and archaeological objects like urns, tombs, and shipwrecks -- which recur across many regions rather than belonging to any single civilization.

sections.name: The Ancient Near East & Middle East
sections.blurb: Mesopotamian cities and empires, Persian dynasties, Phoenician traders, and the rise of Islam across the Levant and Arabia.

entries.note: Bronze Age Anatolian empire, early adopters of iron and chariot warfare; rivaled Egypt at Kadesh.
Capital of the Assyrian Empire under Sennacherib; site of Ashurbanipal's famed library.
Sumerian city-state in Mesopotamia, traditional birthplace of Abraham, noted for its great ziggurat.
Mesopotamian city on the Euphrates, capital under Hammurabi and Nebuchadnezzar II; legendary Hanging Gardens.
Wedge-shaped writing on clay tablets invented by the Sumerians, later used for Akkadian and Persian.
Modern nation spanning Anatolia, successor to the Ottoman Empire, bridging Europe and Asia.
Founder of the Achaemenid Persian Empire who conquered Babylon and freed the Jews from exile.
Central Asian nation on the Silk Road, crossed by Alexander the Great and fought over by great powers.
Achaemenid Persian king who expanded the empire, built Persepolis, and was defeated at Marathon.
Rock-cut Nabataean city in Jordan, wealthy from controlling incense and spice caravan routes.
Last pre-Islamic Persian empire, rival of Byzantium, overthrown by Arab Muslim conquest in the 7th century.
Provincial governors of the Achaemenid Persian Empire who administered regions on the king's behalf.
Nomadic Iranian horse-riding peoples of the Eurasian steppe, known for elaborate gold burial mounds.
Name borne by several Seleucid kings ruling the Hellenistic empire in Syria and Mesopotamia after Alexander.
Semitic peoples of the Arabian Peninsula, unified by Islam and rapid 7th-century conquests.
Mesopotamian empire known for military conquest, capital at Nineveh, fell in 612 BCE.
Phoenician port city in modern Lebanon, source of the word "Bible" via its papyrus trade.
Large Neolithic proto-city settlement in Anatolia, notable for dense mudbrick housing without streets.
Ancient Syrian capital, one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, capital of the Umayyad Caliphate.
Ancient and modern Levantine nation, kingdom of David and Solomon, reestablished as a state in 1948.
Semitic people originating in ancient Israel, dispersed in diaspora after the Roman conquest of Judea.
Holiest city in Islam, birthplace of Muhammad and site of the annual Hajj pilgrimage.
Ancient Iranian people allied with Babylon to destroy Assyria, later absorbed into the Persian Empire.
Bronze Age Hurrian kingdom in northern Mesopotamia and Syria, rival of Egypt and the Hittites.
Founder and prophet of Islam, born in Mecca, who led the Hijra to Medina in 622.
Seafaring Canaanite people from coastal Lebanon who founded Carthage and spread the alphabet.
Phoenician port city famous for purple dye production and for founding Carthage as its colony.
Bronze Age Syrian city whose cuneiform alphabet tablets are among the earliest alphabetic writing.
Sumerian city, one of the world's earliest cities, traditionally linked to the legendary King Gilgamesh.

sections.name: Ancient Egypt & the Nile
sections.blurb: Pharaohs, pyramids, and the Nile Valley civilization from unification under Narmer through Ptolemaic rule.

entries.note: Nile River civilization ruled by pharaohs for three millennia, famed for pyramids and mummification.
Egyptian port city founded by Alexander the Great, home to its famed ancient Library and Lighthouse.
Female pharaoh of the 18th Dynasty who ruled as king and sponsored an expedition to Punt.
Ancient Egyptian religious center sacred to Osiris, site of early royal tombs and Seti I's temple.
Early Egyptian king credited with unifying Upper and Lower Egypt around 3100 BCE, depicted on the Narmer Palette.
Region south of Egypt along the Nile, home to the Kingdom of Kush, alternately conquered by and conquering Egypt.
Third Dynasty pharaoh for whom Imhotep built the Step Pyramid at Saqqara, Egypt's first pyramid.
Semi-legendary trading partner of ancient Egypt, likely in the Horn of Africa, visited by Hatshepsut's expedition.
Macedonian general of Alexander who founded Egypt's Ptolemaic dynasty; name also borne by later Hellenistic pharaohs.

sections.name: Ancient Greece & the Aegean
sections.blurb: City-states, historians, and conquerors of the Greek world, from Sparta and Athens through Alexander's Macedonian empire.

entries.note: Militaristic Greek city-state ruled by dual kings, dependent on helot labor; led the Peloponnesian League.
Enslaved serf class in Sparta, mostly conquered Messenians, who worked the land for Spartan citizens.
Region north of the Aegean inhabited by Thracians, later a Roman province and birthplace of Spartacus.
Athenian lawgiver who reformed debt bondage and laid groundwork for Athenian democracy in the 6th century BCE.
Macedonian king who conquered the Persian Empire and reached India, founding numerous cities named Alexandria.
Wealthy Greek city-state on the isthmus linking mainland Greece to the Peloponnese, destroyed by Rome in 146 BCE.
Anatolian Greek city, birthplace of Herodotus, site of the Mausoleum, one of the Seven Wonders.
Greek historian called the "Father of History," author of The Histories on the Greco-Persian Wars.
Decisive 480 BCE naval battle where a Greek fleet defeated Persian forces under Xerxes.
Heavily armed Greek infantry soldiers who fought in the close-formation phalanx.
Ancient peoples of the western Balkans, frequently at war with Macedon and later Rome.
Circa 1200 BCE societal collapse across the eastern Mediterranean, ending Mycenaean and Hittite civilizations.
Hellenistic Greek kingdom in Anatolia famed for its library and the Pergamon Altar.
King of Macedon who unified Greece under his rule and fathered Alexander the Great.
Greek island famed for the Colossus of Rhodes, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
Legendary city in Anatolia besieged in the Trojan War, excavated by Heinrich Schliemann at Hisarlik.

sections.name: Ancient Rome & the Mediterranean
sections.blurb: Rome's rise from republic to empire, its provinces and rivals across the Mediterranean, and the historians who chronicled it.

entries.note: Phoenician-founded North African city-state that fought Rome in three Punic Wars; destroyed in 146 BCE.
Roman naturalist, author of Natural History, who died observing the eruption of Vesuvius in 79 CE.
Icenian queen who led a revolt against Roman rule in Britain around 60-61 CE, burning Londinium.
Roman emperor who built Hadrian's Wall in Britain and consolidated the empire's frontiers rather than expanding.
Roman name for the Iberian Peninsula, contested in the Punic Wars and later fully annexed by Rome.
Roman emperor infamous for persecuting Christians and blamed for the Great Fire of Rome in 64 CE.
Mediterranean island contested by Carthage and Rome, becoming a Roman province after the First Punic War.
Mediterranean island fought over by Carthage and Rome in the First Punic War, later a Roman province.
Roman name and personification for the province of Britain, conquered under Emperor Claudius.
Roman emperor who legalized Christianity and founded Constantinople as the new capital in 330 CE.
Pre-Roman civilization of central Italy whose culture and rulers heavily influenced early Rome.
Roman general and dictator who conquered Gaul and crossed the Rubicon, triggering civil war.
Roman general and statesman who reformed the army into a professional force; rival of Sulla.
Region covering modern France and Belgium, conquered by Julius Caesar in the Gallic Wars.
Roman name for lands east of the Rhine inhabited by Germanic tribes, never fully conquered.
King of Pontus who fought three wars against Rome, reputedly immune to poison.
Italian city-state that grew into an empire spanning the Mediterranean, from Republic to fall in 476 CE.
Roman imperial dynasty founded by Septimius Severus, a North African-born emperor, in 193 CE.
Roman senator and historian who wrote the Annals and Germania, chronicling early emperors and Germanic tribes.
German classicist and Nobel laureate who wrote the influential History of Rome.
Roman emperor under whom the empire reached its greatest territorial extent, conquering Dacia.

sections.name: East Asia
sections.blurb: Chinese dynasties from Shang through the twentieth century, plus Tibet and the Silk Road networks linking China to the wider world.

entries.note: Island nation with an imperial dynasty that industrialized rapidly in the Meiji era and fought in both World Wars.
East Asian civilization with continuous dynastic history from Shang through Qing, source of silk and Confucianism.
Chinese leader who succeeded Mao and launched market reforms opening China's economy from 1978.
Chinese imperial dynasty following the Qin that expanded the empire and opened the Silk Road.
Chinese dynasty following Mongol Yuan rule, known for the Forbidden City and Zheng He's naval voyages.
Inscribed Shang dynasty divination tools bearing the earliest known Chinese writing.
First imperial Chinese dynasty, under Qin Shi Huang, who unified China and built the Terracotta Army.
Earliest Chinese dynasty with archaeological evidence, known for bronze vessels and oracle bone writing.
Trans-Eurasian trade network connecting China to the Mediterranean, carrying silk, spices, and Buddhism.
Collection of Mao Zedong's quotations distributed widely during China's Cultural Revolution.
Era after the Han dynasty's fall when China split into Wei, Shu, and Wu states.
Himalayan plateau region, center of Tibetan Buddhism, historically autonomous before Chinese incorporation.

sections.name: South & Southeast Asia
sections.blurb: Indus Valley cities, Mauryan and Buddhist India, Sri Lanka, and the colonial and postcolonial states of the region.

entries.note: Mauryan emperor of India who embraced Buddhism after the Kalinga war and issued rock edicts.
Major South Asian river that gave its name to the Bronze Age Indus Valley Civilization.
Island nation off India, formerly Ceylon, a center of Theravada Buddhism and ancient irrigation kingdoms.
Southeast Asian archipelago colonized by Spain then the United States, site of the Philippine-American War.
British trading company that conquered and administered India before the 1858 Crown takeover.
Religion founded by Siddhartha Gautama in India, spread across Asia and promoted by Ashoka.
South Asian subcontinent, home to the Indus Valley, Mauryan, and Mughal civilizations before British colonial rule.
Bronze Age civilization along the Indus River with cities like Mohenjo-daro and Harappa.

sections.name: Sub-Saharan & African History
sections.blurb: Kingdoms, migrations, and colonial-era nations of Africa outside the Nile Valley, from the Garamantes to modern South Africa and Nigeria.

entries.note: Southern African nation, site of Boer republics and apartheid before the Union formed in 1910.
West African nation home to Yoruba, Igbo, and Hausa peoples; former British colony, independent in 1960.
Sub-Saharan language family and migration of peoples that spread agriculture and ironworking across Africa.
Large island off southeast Africa settled by Austronesian and African peoples, former French colony.
Berber civilization of the ancient Sahara in modern Libya, known for advanced underground irrigation.

sections.name: Pre-Columbian & Latin American History
sections.blurb: Mesoamerican and Andean civilizations before Columbus, plus the colonial and modern history of Latin America and the Caribbean.

entries.note: Massive pre-Aztec Mesoamerican city near the Pyramid of the Sun, of uncertain founding people.
Earliest major Mesoamerican civilization, known for colossal stone heads; predecessors of the Maya and Aztecs.
Prehistoric Paleo-Indian culture identified by distinctive fluted stone points found across the Americas.
Seabird excrement mined as fertilizer off Peru and Chile, sparking 19th-century Pacific wars over deposits.
English pirate Edward Teach, active in the Caribbean and American colonies, killed off North Carolina in 1718.
Mesoamerican empire centered at Tenochtitlan, conquered by Hernan Cortes in 1521.
Caribbean island colonized by the British, a key sugar and slave-plantation economy.
Largest South American nation, a Portuguese colony known for sugar, gold, and later coffee economies.
South American nation, source of nitrate and guano wealth, fought the War of the Pacific.
Caribbean island, a Spanish colony until 1898, site of the 1959 Castro revolution.
South American Andean nation named for the equator, part of Gran Colombia after independence from Spain.
1982 conflict between Argentina and the United Kingdom over the South Atlantic islands.
North American nation, site of the Aztec Empire, conquered by Spain, independent since 1821.
Ancient Peruvian culture known for the Nazca Lines, giant geoglyphs etched into the desert.
Peruvian civilization famous for its enormous desert geoglyphs and distinctive polychrome pottery.

sections.name: Oceania & the Pacific
sections.blurb: Indigenous and colonial history of Australia, New Zealand, and the Pacific islands, including Maori-British relations and Antarctic exploration.

entries.note: South Pacific nation settled by Maori voyagers, later a British colony under the Treaty of Waitangi.
Island continent colonized by Britain from 1788 as penal settlements; home to Aboriginal peoples.
Indigenous Polynesian people of New Zealand who signed the Treaty of Waitangi with the British Crown in 1840.
1840 agreement between the British Crown and Maori chiefs establishing British sovereignty over New Zealand.
First British colony in Australia, founded in 1788 at Sydney as a penal settlement.
Indigenous peoples of Australia with one of the world's oldest continuous cultures, dispossessed by British colonization.
Watercraft central to Polynesian voyaging and the settlement of the Pacific islands.
British explorer of Antarctica who led the Endurance expedition; his ship was crushed by ice in 1915.
Pacific island nation, site of US nuclear weapons testing at Bikini Atoll after WWII.
Australian city in Victoria, founded in 1835, host of the 1956 Summer Olympics.
Series of intertribal Maori conflicts in New Zealand fought with European firearms in the early 1800s.
Head of government position in Australia's parliamentary system since federation in 1901.
Pacific island nation, a former British colony independent since 1970, home to Indigenous Fijians and Indo-Fijians.
Conservative New Zealand prime minister of the late 1970s-80s, known for economic controls and the 1981 Springbok tour crisis.

sections.name: Canada & Northern North America
sections.blurb: Canadian confederation, its provinces and prime ministers, and the indigenous peoples of the Arctic north.

entries.note: British North American dominion formed by the 1867 confederation of former French and British colonies.
Pacific coast Canadian city in British Columbia, named for explorer George Vancouver.
Northwestern North American territory purchased by the United States from Russia in 1867.
Canadian prime minister and diplomat who won the 1957 Nobel Peace Prize for mediating the Suez Crisis.
Maritime Canadian province, site of early French Acadian settlement and the British fortress of Louisbourg.
Early 20th-century ban on the manufacture and sale of alcohol, enacted across the US and Canadian provinces.
Ancient Inuit culture that spread across Arctic Canada and Greenland, ancestors of modern Inuit peoples.

sections.name: Modern Europe & the World Wars
sections.blurb: Nineteenth- and twentieth-century European states, the two World Wars, and the rise and fall of the Soviet Union.

entries.note: Central European nation unified in 1871; central belligerent in both twentieth-century World Wars.
Western European nation, major WWI belligerent alongside Britain and Russia against Germany.
1914-1918 global conflict triggered by the assassination of Franz Ferdinand; ended the Ottoman and Russian empires.
Island nation that gained independence from Britain in 1922 after centuries of British rule.
Decade of Cold War tension, the Falklands War, and the start of Deng Xiaoping's market reforms in China.
Union centered on Britain, head of a global empire and belligerent in both World Wars.
German capital, divided during the Cold War, site of the 1989 Wall's fall.
Balkan nation, a Central Power ally in WWI, later a Soviet-bloc communist state.
Compulsory military service; mass mobilization policy that expanded dramatically in WWI-era Europe.
European nation whose maritime empire once spanned Brazil, Africa, and Asia.
Detection technology developed by Britain before WWII, decisive in the Battle of Britain.
Balkan nation that joined WWI against the Central Powers, later a Soviet-bloc state under Ceausescu.
Eurasian nation, an imperial power under the Tsars until the 1917 Revolution ended Romanov rule.
Northern part of Great Britain, united with England by the 1707 Act of Union.
Communist state formed after the 1917 Russian Revolution, dissolved in 1991.
Early medieval Anglo-Saxon ship burial site in England containing a ceremonial helmet and treasure hoard.
Bolshevik revolutionary leader who founded the Soviet Union after the 1917 October Revolution.

sections.name: Trade, Technology & Material Culture
sections.blurb: Common-link commodities, materials, and archaeological objects, from metals and crops to artifacts, traded and used across many civilizations.

entries.note: Prized green stone carved for ritual objects in ancient China and Mesoamerica alike.
Cartographic documents; common-link topic spanning ancient geography like Ptolemy's work to the Age of Exploration.
Metal currency; common-link topic from Lydian invention through Roman and Chinese minting traditions.
Metal whose smelting ended the Bronze Age; associated with Hittite technology and the Iron Age.
Metal alloyed with copper to make bronze; its scarcity drove long-distance Bronze Age trade.
Wheeled horse-drawn vehicles central to Bronze Age warfare among Hittites, Egyptians, and Mycenaeans.
Stimulant crop originating in Ethiopia, later cultivated across Arabia, Latin America, and colonial Southeast Asia.
Historiographical method examining a small-scale subject, like a village or trial, to illuminate broader trends.
Common-link theme covering irrigation, aqueducts, and maritime trade routes across civilizations.
Alloy of copper and tin that defined the Bronze Age across the Near East, Mediterranean, and China.
Domesticated pack animals central to trans-Saharan and Silk Road caravan trade.
Early-smelted metal, alloyed with tin for bronze, mined across Cyprus and the ancient Near East.
Precious metal driving ancient trade routes and colonial ventures, from Nubia's mines to the Spanish conquest of the Americas.
Narcotic crop at the center of the 19th-century Opium Wars between Britain and Qing China.
Tyrian purple dye extracted from murex snails, prized in antiquity and named for Phoenician production centers.
Latex commodity from the Amazon and later the Congo and Southeast Asia, driving colonial exploitation.
Preservative mineral traded via trans-Saharan routes and taxed under monopolies like the one Gandhi's Salt March targeted.
Fired clay material used for China's Terracotta Army as well as Greek pottery and figurines.
Vessels used across cultures for storage and funerary ash burial, closely studied by archaeologists.
Fermented grape beverage central to Mediterranean trade and culture from ancient Greece and Rome to Gaul.
Discipline of excavating and studying the material remains of past human societies.
Tropical fruit crop associated with Central American "banana republics" and the United Fruit Company.
British bat-and-ball sport spread throughout the empire, notably to Australia, India, and the Caribbean.
Practice of predicting the future, historically via oracle bones, entrails, or astrology across cultures.
Domesticated animal that revolutionized warfare and transport across Eurasia and later the Americas.
Archaeological period following the Bronze Age marked by widespread iron tool and weapon use.
Catholic religious order active in missionary work across China, Japan, and colonial Latin America.
Common-link theme covering land tenure, reform, and ownership disputes across societies.
Staple grain crop foundational to agriculture across East and Southeast Asia.
Domesticated livestock central to pastoral economies and the wool trade in ancient and colonial societies.
Used historically as currency (cowries) and ornamentation across African, Pacific, and Native American trade.
Sunken vessels studied by underwater archaeologists to reconstruct ancient trade and seafaring.
Luxury textile originating in China, the namesake commodity of the trans-Eurasian Silk Road.
Ancient North African plant prized by Cyrene for medicinal and contraceptive use; harvested to extinction.
Beverage crop originating in China, central to British colonial trade with India and the Opium Wars.
Burial structures studied archaeologically across Egyptian, Chinese, and other ancient cultures.
Refers to prehistoric Paleolithic carved female figurines found across Europe, presumed fertility or religious symbols.
Marine mammals hunted historically for oil and baleen, driving the 19th-century whaling industry.
Alleged practitioners of malevolent magic, subject to historical persecutions like the European witch trials.
Building and shipbuilding material whose scarcity shaped naval power, as with the cedar of Lebanon or English oak.
